title: Central clearing and the pricing of specialness in repo markets
summary: This ECB Working Paper develops a theoretical model of security-driven repo markets examining how OTC bilateral clearing and CCP clearing price counterparty risk differently. The model shows repo rates are non-linear in borrower default risk, causing the average OTC rate to be more negative than the CCP rate because OTC markets price borrower-specific risk individually while CCP markets pool and average counterparty risk. Empirical analysis using euro-area interbank repo market data around the March 2020 COVID-19 shock finds the CCP-OTC specialness differential compresses by roughly 6 basis points during stress.
key_points: Repo markets in Europe operate through bilateral OTC clearing where counterparties know each other's identity, or through CCP clearing where trades are anonymous and counterparty risk is pooled via a default fund
The model predicts that in normal times, special securities trade at more negative rates in OTC markets than CCP markets due to non-linear pricing of borrower-specific risk
The CCP-OTC differential compresses during periods of counterparty uncertainty such as the COVID-19 shock, as pooling risk becomes relatively more costly in CCP markets
Empirical evidence from March 2020 COVID-19 shock shows CCP borrowing costs increased relative to OTC markets by approximately 6 basis points
The compression in CCP-OTC differential is weaker for riskier borrowers (measured by NPL ratios and CDS spreads) and stronger for high-quality collateral (core euro-area government bonds)
Central clearing dampens specialness in normal times but helps stabilize pricing during stress by reducing dispersion in borrower-specific rates
why_it_matters: This research is directly relevant to central banks and regulators assessing central clearing policies. The findings reveal a fundamental trade-off in market design: identity-based bilateral OTC markets allow granular risk-sensitive pricing but may be more fragile during stress, while pooled anonymous CCP markets reduce price dispersion and act as shock absorbers. For the ECB specifically, understanding these mechanisms is crucial for monetary policy transmission and collateral framework operations, as CCPs affect both the resilience of funding markets and the equilibrium price of scarce collateral.
market_structure_context: The paper examines the structural differences between OTC and CCP clearing in euro-area interbank repo markets, showing how clearing venue choice fundamentally affects collateral pricing. The key insight is that CCPs introduce anonymity and counterparty pooling that changes the pricing function from borrower-specific to pooled-average rates, compressing specialness in normal times but stabilizing pricing during stress. This has direct implications for understanding repo market plumbing and financial market infrastructure design.
important_numbers: 6 basis points CCP-OTC differential compression during COVID-19 stress
